title: Chapter 2: The Hallway Girl
content: The door opened three inches and the smell hit me like a wall.

Decay. Copper. Something sweet underneath it all, like fruit left to rot in the sun. My stomach heaved, but I'd skipped breakfast—the boss fight—so there was nothing to bring up. I pressed my sleeve over my nose and peered through the gap.

The hallway stretched in both directions, dim and trashed. Overhead lights were dead, the only illumination from the afternoon sun bleeding through the stairwell window at the far end. The carpet was dark with stains I refused to identify. Mr. Patterson and the woman from 12C were gone—shambled off to some other floor, maybe.
The girl was still there.

Four meters away, the watch had said. She was standing exactly where I'd last seen her, outside 12D, motionless as a photograph. The sunlight from the stairwell didn't quite reach her. She stood in shadow, that black hair framing a face that belonged on a magazine cover, not in a destroyed hallway in a dead building.
My hand trembled on the door.
**QUEST CONDITION: Make physical contact with the target and speak to her with genuine emotional intent**
"Genuine emotional intent," I muttered. "What does that even mean? She's a zombie. She doesn't have emotions. I don't have emotions. We're a perfect match."
My own joke made me want to cry.

I opened the door a little wider and slipped through, pulling it shut behind me but not locking it. Escape route. Always have an escape route. That was basic gaming logic, and right now it was the only logic I had.
The girl didn't react. I took a step. Then another. The carpet squelched under my sneakers and I nearly turned back, but—
Three meters.
Her head turned.
Just slightly. A fraction of an inch. Her eyes—those huge, pupil-blown eyes—tracked me like a cat watching a moth.
Two meters.

I could see her breathing. Zombies weren't supposed to breathe, not like this—shallow, rhythmic, the rise and fall of a living chest. Her lips were parted, slightly blue, and I could hear the faintest sound coming from her throat. Not a moan. Something softer. Almost like...
A hum.
One meter.
"Hi," I said. My voice cracked on the syllable like I was thirteen again, talking to Lisa Park in seventh-grade English class.
Her head tilted. The humming stopped. Those dead eyes—no, not dead, not quite—locked onto my face with an intensity that made every hair on my arms stand up.
I reached out my hand.
It was shaking. Obviously it was shaking. I was reaching for a zombie in a hallway that stank of death because a magic watch on my wrist told me to. My hand—
My fingertips touched the back of her hand.
Cold. Ice cold. But the skin was intact, smooth, human.

**QUEST CONDITION MET: Physical contact ✓**
**REMAINING: Speak with genuine emotional intent**
I stared at her. She stared at me. Her fingers curled—slowly, mechanically—around my hand. Not squeezing. Not attacking. Just... holding.
"What's your name?" I whispered.
She didn't answer. Her jaw worked, soundlessly, like a fish pulled from water.

"You're beautiful," I said. And I meant it—God help me, I meant it. In a world of screaming and rot and the end of everything, she was beautiful, standing there in her silk top with the last light of the sun catching her hair, looking at me like I was the first real thing she'd seen since she'd died.
"I'm Alex," I said. "I live in 12B. I'm not going to hurt you."
Her fingers tightened around mine.

**QUEST UPDATE: FIRST ROMANCE — CONDITION MET**
**TARGET RESPONSE: RECEPTIVE (Emotional Resonance Detected)**
**AFFECTION LEVEL: 5%**
The watch buzzed, and a small compartment I hadn't noticed slid open on its side. Inside, wrapped in what looked like sterile plastic, was a package labeled in glowing blue text:

**FRESH RAW MEAT — 2kg**
**PURIFIED WATER — 5L**

I pulled the package out with trembling hands. Real. Solid. Cold from some impossible refrigeration, the plastic beaded with condensation. I looked at the meat—actual raw beef, marbled and red, the kind that cost thirty dollars a pound at the Whole Foods I'd never shopped at because who has that kind of money?
My stomach screamed at me. I wanted to eat it right there, raw, on the carpet, zombie girl be damned.
But she was watching the meat. Her nostrils flared. Her grip on my hand tightened, and the sound from her throat changed—from that soft hum to something urgent, wanting.
"You're hungry," I said.
Of course she was hungry. Zombies were always hungry.
I opened the plastic with my teeth—the cleanest tool available—and tore off a strip of raw beef. I held it up, our hands still intertwined, and offered it to her like I was feeding a stray cat.
She lunged.

Not at me. At the meat. Her free hand shot out with a speed that made my heart stop, and she snatched the beef from my fingers with a grip that could have crushed stone. She shoved it into her mouth, chewing messily, desperately, and a sound came out of her that I'd never heard from a zombie—
She made a noise of satisfaction.
Not a moan. Not a growl. A sound of *pleasure*.

I fed her the rest of the meat, piece by piece, my hand in hers the entire time. The water I poured into a cracked coffee mug I found on the hallway floor—someone's abandoned morning—and she drank from it like a person. Not like a monster. Like a person who was desperately, achingly thirsty.
When the meat was gone and the water was finished, she looked at me.
Really looked at me.
Her pupils contracted. Just a fraction—not the blown discs of a predator but something approaching *focus*. Her lips moved.
No sound came out.
But I could read them. I'd spent enough time reading subtitles to recognize the shape.
*Stay.*
"What?" I breathed.
Her fingers squeezed mine again. Her lips moved once more.
*Stay.*
"Okay," I said. "Okay. I'll stay."

**ROMANCE SYSTEM — TARGET: UNNAMED (Designation: LUNA)**
**AFFECTION: 8%**
**NOTE: Target has responded to feeding with unusual cognitive activity. Full name unknown. Assigning designation LUNA.**

I sat down on the hallway floor, my back against Apartment 12D's door, and Luna sat down beside me. She was still cold. She still smelled faintly of death. Her hand was still wrapped around mine, her grip strong enough to break bone but gentle enough to hold a soap bubble.
The sun moved across the stairwell window and cast long shadows through the hall.
We sat together in the silence of the dead building, a gamer and a zombie, and I realized with a jolt that this was the first time I'd held anyone's hand since my mother's, five years ago.
I didn't let go.
She didn't either.
